1: On the yom hashemini (yôm haššᵉmînî), the eighth day — the number eight in biblical numerology signals a new beginning beyond the seven of completion; circumcision on the eighth day (Leviticus 12:3), resurrection on the first/eighth day of the week. The eighth day of priestly ordination is the dawn of the new era of active ministry after the seven-day formation; the new creation begins when consecration is complete.
2: Aaron is instructed to bring a ben baqar (ben bāqār, a young bull/calf) for his sin offering — the same category of animal as his ordination sin offering (Leviticus 8:14); the high priest's inaugural service begins where his ordination began: with his own sin offering. The permanent structural need of the Aaronic high priest for personal atonement before every service is Hebrews' central argument for Christ's superiority (Hebrews 7:27-28).
3: The people bring a male goat (śᵉʿîr ʿizzîm) for a sin offering and a calf and a lamb for burnt offerings — the full suite of atonement and devotion offerings for the community on the inaugural day of priestly service. The variety of animals and offerings reflects the comprehensive scope of the relationship being inaugurated: sin covered, total devotion expressed.
4: The shelem (šᵉlāmîm, peace/fellowship offering; from šālôm) is the culminating offering of the inaugural day — the peace offering is the goal of the entire sacrificial system: atonement secured (sin offering) + devotion expressed (burnt offering) = fellowship enjoyed (peace offering). “YHWH will appear to you” (niraʾ ʾalêkem YHWH) is the stated purpose; all the rituals exist for the sake of the divine appearance.
5: All the congregation drew near and stood before YHWH — qarov (qārᵃbû, they drew near); the technical verb of priestly approach is here applied to the entire congregation; the priestly ritual brings the whole community into YHWH's presence, not just the priest. The corporate character of Israel's worship: the individual priest mediates for the whole, who are themselves drawn near.
6: “That the kevod YHWH (kᵉbôd YHWH) may appear to you” — the glory of YHWH is the stated goal of the entire day's ritual. The kāvôd (weighty presence, glory) is what the tabernacle, priesthood, and sacrifice all exist to mediate. John 1:14: “we have seen his glory, glory as of the only Son from the Father” — the Incarnation is the ultimate appearance of the divine glory that Lev 9 promises.
7: “Qarav el hamizbeach” (qᵉrav ʾel-hammizbêaḥ): “draw near to the altar” — Aaron's first official act is the technical priestly motion of approach. Qarav (to draw near/approach) is the defining verb of priestly worship throughout Leviticus; the priest is the one who is permitted to draw near where others would die. Hebrews 4:16: “let us therefore draw near (proserchōmetha) with confidence to the throne of grace” — the new covenant opens to all what the altar of Lev 9 permitted only to Aaron.
8: Aaron drew near to the altar and killed the calf of his own sin offering — his first official act as functioning high priest is self-atonement; the inaugural ministry begins with personal sin offering before any act on the people's behalf. The logic of Hebrews 7:27 is inescapable: the Aaronic high priest must always begin with himself; Christ began with himself as the offering, not as one needing the offering.
9: Aaron dipped his finger in the blood and put it on the horns of the altar (qarnôt hammizbêaḥ) and poured the rest at the base — the finger-dab application of sin-offering blood to the four altar horns is the distinctive application pattern of the sin offering; the horns (power-points) of the altar are the location where atonement blood is applied, and the base-pour returns excess blood to the ground.
10: The fat portions (ḥēlev), kidneys, and liver lobe were burned on the altar as YHWH commanded — the prescribed portions for YHWH from every offering; burning these choice pieces on the altar is the act of giving the best to God. Psalms 63:5: “my soul will be satisfied as with fat and rich food” — the ḥēlev is the OT's symbol of the richest blessing; giving it to YHWH is offering the finest.
11: The flesh and skin of the sin offering bull were burned outside the camp — the sin offering whose blood was not brought into the inner sanctuary is disposed of outside the camp (cf. 6:30 where offerings whose blood enters the sanctuary are burned inside; Leviticus 10:17-18 revisits this distinction). Hebrews 13:11-13 interprets this type: “Jesus also suffered outside the gate.”
12: Aaron killed the burnt offering (ʿōlāh) and his sons dashed the blood against the sides of the altar — the transition from sin offering (atonement) to burnt offering (devotion) follows the prescribed sequence: first make atonement, then present total devotion. The order is theologically determinative: the cross (atonement) precedes the consecrated life (devotion).
13: Aaron's sons handed him the burnt offering piece by piece and the head — the systematic dismemberment and burning of every part of the burnt offering; piece by piece, nothing held back; thorough and complete. The total devotion of the burnt offering is the OT's image of what Romans 12:1 commands: “present your bodies as a living sacrifice.”
14: Aaron washed the entrails and legs with water before burning them on the altar — the inward parts must be clean before being offered; the offering is both outwardly and inwardly pure. Christ, the true burnt offering, was pure within and without: “holy, innocent, unstained” (Hebrews 7:26); his inner life matched his outer righteousness.
15: Aaron offered the people's sin offering goat and made atonement for the people — the priestly service for the community follows the priestly service for the self. Aaron first atoned for himself (vv 8-11), then turns to the people. Hebrews 5:3: “because of this he is obligated to offer sacrifice for his own sins just as he does for those of the people” — the Aaronic sequence that Christ's priesthood transcends.
16: Aaron presented the burnt offering kashmishpat (kammišpāṭ): “according to the rule/ordinance” — the offering is validated by its conformity to the prescribed procedure; the rule is not an obstacle but the form of acceptable worship. Jesus: “in accordance with the Scriptures” (1 Corinthians 15:3-4) — his death and resurrection are likewise validated by conformity to the divine prescription.
17: Aaron presented the grain offering (minḥāh), took a handful, and burned it on the altar alongside the morning burnt offering — the grain offering expresses devotion through the work of human hands (harvested, ground, baked, offered); it complements the animal offering by adding the human-labor dimension. The full offering combines animal blood and human agricultural labor — the whole created order in service to God.
18: Aaron slaughtered the ox and ram as shelamim (šᵉlāmîm, peace/fellowship offerings) for the people — the peace offering is the goal and culmination of the entire sequence; its name from šālôm (wholeness, peace, well-being) names what it effects: restored relationship, fellowship, and wholeness between Israel and YHWH. The eschatological banquet (Revelation 19:9) is the ultimate peace offering — the meal of permanent fellowship.
19: Aaron's sons handed him the blood of the peace offering, and he dashed it against the sides of the altar all around — the vigorous blood-dash of the peace offering completes the blood-ritual of the day; blood marks every altar-surface as the meeting point of divine holiness and human sinfulness resolved by sacrifice.
20: The fat pieces were placed on the breasts and burned on the altar — YHWH's portion (the fat) and the priestly portion (the breast) are both presented before YHWH before being distributed; the waving and burning that follows (v21) enacts the exchange between YHWH and his priests.
21: Aaron waved the chazeh hatenufa (ḥāzeh hattᵉnûpāh, wave breast) and the shoq hayamin (šôq hayāmîn, right thigh) as a wave offering — the breast and right thigh are the priests' statutory portions from every peace offering; the wave offering presents them to YHWH and receives them back as his gift to the priests. The workers are sustained by the work they serve; 1 Timothy 5:18: “the laborer deserves his wages.”
22: Aaron lifted his hands toward the people and blessed them (wayyiśśāʾ ʾahărōn ʾet yādāyw ʾel hāʿām waybārᵃkēm) — the high-priestly benediction is the culmination of the entire day's service; the Aaronic blessing (Numbers 6:24-26) is the priest's primary gift to the people. Luke 24:50-51: Jesus “lifting up his hands he blessed them. While he blessed them, he parted from them” — the ascending Christ performs the high-priestly benediction over his disciples, the new covenant fulfillment of Aaron's lifted hands.
23: “The kevod YHWH (kᵉbôd YHWH) appeared to all the people” — the stated goal of v6 is achieved; YHWH manifests his presence in visible glory to the entire congregation. The divine glory appearing to “all the people” democratizes the theophany; ordinarily the divine presence is restricted (only the high priest enters the most holy place); here it is publicly manifested. John 1:14: “we have seen his glory” — the Incarnation is the ultimate democratic theophany, the divine glory made publicly visible.
24: Vatetzei esh milifnei YHWH (wattēṣēʾ ʾēš milᵉpānê YHWH): “fire came out from before YHWH” and consumed the burnt offering — the theophanic fire is YHWH's sign of acceptance, the same fire that later consumes Nadab and Abihu for unauthorized worship (Leviticus 10:2); the same divine fire blesses complete obedience and destroys presumptuous deviation. The people's response: they shouted (yāronnû) and fell on their faces (wayyippeḷû ʿal pᵉnêhem) — the mixed response of joy and awe before the holy God. Hebrews 12:28-29: “our God is a consuming fire.”
